items.q: Why do manufacturers use jigs and fixtures?
items.correct: They locate the work and guide tools so each repeated operation is identical, giving batch accuracy and speed without marking out every individual part.
items.misconception: Jigs are only needed for one-off craft products made by hand.
items.random_wrong: A jig is the rotating chuck that grips work in a lathe.
items.why: Students associate workshop aids with hand craft, but jigs matter most in batch production, where repeatability and time saving multiply across the run.

items.q: Does design for manufacture mean making a product as advanced as possible?
items.correct: No — it means simplifying for production: fewer parts, standard components, processes matched to the production scale, reducing cost, time and assembly errors.
items.misconception: DFM means packing in advanced features to impress customers.
items.random_wrong: DFM is a law requiring products to be made in the country of sale.
items.why: Students read 'for manufacture' as 'high-tech'; the principle is the opposite — strip complexity out so manufacture becomes easier and cheaper.

items.q: On a Gantt chart, what do overlapping bars show?
items.correct: Tasks scheduled to run concurrently — in parallel — which shortens the overall project compared with doing every task in sequence.
items.misconception: Tasks on a Gantt chart must always run one after another, so bars never overlap.
items.random_wrong: Overlapping bars show tasks that have gone over budget.
items.why: Students assume planning is purely sequential; spotting which tasks can run in parallel is precisely how project managers compress timescales.

items.q: What is a tolerance on an engineering drawing?
items.correct: The allowable variation in a dimension
items.misconception: The hardness of the specified material
items.random_wrong: The expected lifespan of the product
items.why: It's The allowable variation in a dimension. “The hardness of the specified material” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What is the purpose of a jig?
items.correct: To guide a tool or hold work so repeated operations are identical
items.why: It's To guide a tool or hold work so repeated operations are identical. “To measure surface finish” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which statement correctly distinguishes QA from QC?
items.correct: QA is the system preventing defects; QC inspects outputs against the specification
items.misconception: QA happens after sale; QC happens before design
items.random_wrong: QA tests prototypes; QC writes the design brief
items.why: The answer examiners want is: QA is the system preventing defects; QC inspects outputs against the specification. “QA happens after sale; QC happens before design” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Why do manufacturers use standardised bought-in components such as hinges?
items.correct: They are cheaper and more reliable than making equivalents in-house
items.misconception: They make every product look unique
items.random_wrong: They avoid the need for quality checks
items.why: The answer examiners want is: They are cheaper and more reliable than making equivalents in-house. “They make every product look unique” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A dimension is 25 mm with a tolerance of plus or minus 0.2 mm. What is the minimum acceptable size?
items.why: Exact answer: 24.8 mm. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
